summary: Decompiled item function it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk in src/melee/it/items/itnessyoyo.c by replacing a MWERKS_GEKKO inline-assembly implementation and NOT_IMPLEMENTED fallback with C. The resulting C calls it_8026B894 and preserves an apparently unused comparison against the Ness yo-yo item owner field using a volatile pointer expression.

key_files.role: Only changed file; replaced inline assembly/NOT_IMPLEMENTED for it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk with C.

what_changed.text: Removed the MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly block for it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk, including pragma push/pop and the NOT_IMPLEMENTED non-Gekko fallback.
Added a C body that gets the Item with GET_ITEM(gobj), calls it_8026B894(gobj, ref_gobj), then evaluates a comparison between ip->xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 and ref_gobj through a volatile owner_ptr.
The PR body states that an unused comparison was the tricky part and that the function matched 100% on the author's end.
The automated GALE01 report comment says 'No changes'.

smart_moves.text: The decompilation kept the otherwise-unused comparison visible in C rather than deleting it, matching the assembly sequence where the result of cmplw is not consumed before function exit.
Used the typed item variable path ip->xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 instead of a raw offset for the former lwz at 0xDE4.

decomp_lessons.lesson: When assembly performs a compare whose condition result is not used, matching C may still need to preserve the load/compare shape with a deliberate no-op expression rather than simplifying it away.
A volatile pointer to a typed field can be used narrowly to keep a needed load/compare from being optimized away, but this is a matching tactic that should remain tied to objdiff or report evidence.

naming_conventions.name: owner_ptr
naming_conventions.meaning: local volatile pointer to the Ness yo-yo item variable field x10, compared with ref_gobj

assembly_or_matching_tactics.tactic: Replace inline assembly with C that preserves a dead comparison through `(void) ((*owner_ptr == ref_gobj) ? 0 : 0);`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics.notes: The author explicitly identified the unused comparison as tricky. The bot report says 'No changes', but the slice does not include an objdiff transcript.

assembly_or_matching_tactics.tactic: Use `HSD_GObj* volatile* owner_ptr = &ip->xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10;` to force a field load and comparison without raw pointer arithmetic.
assembly_or_matching_tactics.notes: This is supported by the diff but should not be generalized without verifier evidence in future PRs.

notes: Changed file and code diff are complete and concrete.
There are no review comments or human review feedback in the slice.
The PR body claims a 100% match on the author's end, and the bot report says 'No changes', but no full objdiff/checkdiff log is included.
The broader semantic meaning of the Ness yo-yo x10 field is not proven by this slice; only the local owner_ptr role in the new code is supported.

accepted_candidate_records.title: Preserve unused compare sequences when matching
accepted_candidate_records.text: In PR #2545, the assembly for it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk loaded ip->xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 and compared it to ref_gobj even though the comparison result was not consumed. The matching C preserved that operation with a volatile pointer and a void no-op ternary expression.

source_update_candidates.title: Document volatile no-op comparisons as narrow matching tactics
source_update_candidates.text: Consider adding a note that dead loads or compares visible in assembly may require a narrow volatile/no-op expression to preserve codegen, but only with explicit verifier evidence and without treating the pattern as normal source style.
source_update_candidates.reason: PR #2545 provides a concrete example where an unused comparison was the known matching obstacle, but the tactic should remain evidence-gated.

rejection_notes: Do not infer that xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 is definitively named or semantically an owner field beyond the local variable name owner_ptr used in this PR.
Do not promote the volatile no-op ternary expression as preferred style; the evidence only supports it as a narrow matching tactic for this function.
Do not claim reviewer approval details; the slice contains no review comments.
